01a_quotes.title: When GenAI increases inequality: evidence from a university debating competition

study_design: I run a randomized controlled trial (RCT) to evaluate the e ects of ChatGPT (the most widely used GenAI system) on debating performance in a university debating competition, involving 142 undergraduate students.

Randomization was done in situ , using a STATA command, at the individual level on each of the three debating days. The randomization took three steps: First, participants were randomly assigned to treatment and control groups. Second, participants were randomly assigned to debating partners in each of the four rounds. Third, participants were randomly assigned debating positions in each round.

The debate contest followed a simpliﬁed version of the "British Parliament" style competition, consisting of three to four rounds of short, one-on-one debates. After the ﬁrst round of debates (the baseline), half of the students - the treatment group - were randomly assigned a 20-minute intense training of ChatGPT and were allowed to use it as support throughout the contest. The control group could only use conventional resources on the internet.
Each debate was audio recorded and sent to three di erent independent expert judges that did not have any information about the experiment.
The experiment was presented to participants as a debating competition with the aim of testing the impact of di erent technological tools in debates.
task: The challenge consisted in three to four rounds of short one-on-one debates on public policy topics over a three-hour session.

In my experiment, for evaluation purposes, I chose to do individual debates rather than group debates, as commonly done in BP debates. Also, given limited class-time slots o ered by professors, I shortened the length of debates to 3+2 minutes per debater: each student had three minutes for an opening statement and two minutes for refutation and conclusion. Preparation time for each round of debates was 15 minutes.
Students were asked to bring their computers and cellphones to the competition and did not receive any materials in advance to prepare the debates.
At the end of the debating competition, each student had done three or four rounds of debates. In total, each student had about two hours of debating time, involving preparation and actual debates.

One of the clearly relevant applications of ChatGPT is that it provides very fast summaries of complex ideas and concepts, arguments in favor or against any topic and endless examples and metaphors. For these reasons, ChatGPT can be a powerful ally for improving debating skills.
population: The intervention took place at ESADE Business School and at CUNEF University over three di erent debating days. The two ﬁrst sessions, with 38 and 50 students registered respectively, where organised at ESADE on the 20th and 24th of March 2023. The third session, with 58 participants, was organized on the 19th of April 2023. The target population were undergraduate university students. Most participants were from three courses: two Debating courses at Esade and an Economic Policy class at CUNEF. Most students were enrolled in either law, business or economics degrees.
The ﬁrst session was run in English, the other two sessions in Spanish.
At the moment of the intervention, ChatGPT3.5 had been out for about four months. Less than half of participating students declared having used ChatGPT before.

Table 1 shows balancing in baseline characteristics between treatment and control group individuals. Randomization was balanced with respect to the information collected at baseline, which included basic socio-demographic characteristics, academic achievements, previous experience in debating competitions or courses, preferred language for debates and previous experience in using ChatGPT, among other variables.
arms: After the ﬁrst round, treatment students were sent to a separate room and received a 20-minute training on ChatGPT by a ChatGPT heavy user. Control students were given an extra 20-minute break.

Treatment and control students remained separated in two rooms and were given 45 minutes to prepare the three following debates. AI tools were explicitly prohibited for control students. Treatment students were explicitly told not to tell about ChatGPT.

Given that participants were randomly assigned di erent tools for debating, prizes were given according to the tools used: ﬁve prizes to top performers in the treatment group and ﬁve in the control group.
In the ﬁrst round of debates ChatGPT was prohibited for everyone.
outcome_definitions: There are two main outcome variables: individual debating points and winning the debate.

For each student, each observation is a ﬁve minute (3+2) recording of his two interventions in each round of debates. The recorded debates were sent for evaluation to three di erent judges (see Section 2.4) . Judges were asked to give up to 50 points to each debater following a rubric ﬁve indicators typically used in debating competitions (see Annex A.5).

In order to construct my main outcome variable, individual debating points, I compute the average points given by the three judges to each participant, separately for each of the three to four rounds of debates. I classify an individual as winner of the debate if their average score (across three judges) in a debate was higher than that of their their rival.

Judges were asked to provide two sets of ﬁnal outcomes: total debating points of each participant and winner of the debate. Every debate was evaluated by three di erent judges in order to reduce the probability of results being explained by potential judge biases. To calculate the ﬁnal scores, I computed the average of the three evaluations for each debate.

They had to give ten points to each participant in ﬁve standard categories: (1) clarity and validity of the defended position; (2) credibility of the evidence used; (3) formal quality and rhetoric; (4) ability to refute the rival's arguments and (5) superiority of the arguments used (see Annex A.5 for more details).
statistical_results: I ﬁnd that ChatGPT has a positive but not signiﬁcant e ect on overall debating performance. Treatment debaters are 9.2% more likely to win than control debaters and score on average 2.2% (equivalent to an increase by 0.15 standard deviations) higher than control individuals, but the di erences are not statistically di erent from zero.

In Column 1, which estimates equation 1 and includes debate day and round ﬁxed e ects, and controls for baseline performance, treatment group individuals score 0.518 points higher than control individuals. When including additional controls (Column 2), the coe cient increases to 0.672, but remains imprecisely estimated and is not signiﬁcant at conventional levels.

When looking at "winning the debate" (Columns 3 and 4), ChatGPT increases the probability of winning by between 3.9 and 4.6 percentage points, but neither of these estimates is signiﬁcant at conventional levels.

Treatment debaters score on average 2.2% higher (equivalent to an increase by 0.15 standard deviations) than control individuals, and they are 9.2% more likely to win, but neither of the results is signiﬁcant.
The coe cient estimate shows an improvement of 12% among treatment students who are on a scholarship, and a 1.7% (insigniﬁcant) e ect of ChatGPT among those without a merit scholarship.

I ﬁnd that students on such scholarships experience signiﬁcantly larger improvements from ChatGPT than those without a scholarship. They improve their total debating points by 3.1 points compared to control students, while those without a scholarship just show a small positive but insigniﬁcant improvement.

The coe cient estimate represents a signiﬁcant improvement by 11.8% for students using ChatGPT among those on a scholarship, and a 1.5% (insigniﬁcant) e ect of ChatGPT among those without a scholarship.

High ability treated students are also signiﬁcantly more likely to win the debate (even though the coe cient becomes insigniﬁcant in the speciﬁcation with controls, column 4), whereas their low-ability counterparts do not beneﬁt from the treatment in this respect.

I ﬁnd that those with high scores at baseline (top 50% of the debate performance distribution) beneﬁt from ChatGPT - they improve their total debating points by 2.36 points (equivalent to a 0.8 standard deviation increase) compared to control students-, while those with lower baseline debate points (bottom 50%) do not beneﬁt at all.

Among those in the top 50% in debating points in the baseline round (before the treatment was implemented), treatment students have on average 5.2% higher points than control students - the equivalent of a 0.47 standard deviation increase compared to control students-. Among those with lower baseline debating points (bottom 50%), treatment individuals do not beneﬁt at all from ChatGPT.

ChatGPT signiﬁcantly boosts high-ability participants' productivity in four out of ﬁve dimensions, including credibility and superiority of the arguments and refutation and rhetoric capacity (when pooled together, the overall e ect in credibility and rhetoric is neutralized by the low-ability students).
The indicator shows a very strong e ect of ChatGPT for high-ability students and a negative (but insigniﬁcant) e ect for low-ability students.
low-skill seem to signiﬁcantly beneﬁt from ChatGPT in one speciﬁc task: improving the clarity of their defended position.

I ﬁnd a positive and signiﬁcant e ect of ChatGPT on self-reported perception of time needed to prepare the debates, but only for high-ability individuals: they self-rate the su ciency of time 31 points (or 1.06 standard deviations) higher than control individuals.

I also ﬁnd a positive e ect of the treatment on students' perceptions of the usefulness of the tools used for the debate: among those not on a scholarship, treatment individuals value the usefulness of materials 36 points higher than students having only internet access (or 1.07 standard deviations), and the e ect was similar in size among those on a scholarship (albeit not signiﬁcant).

When individuals had to defend the strong evidence position, treatment had a positive e ect and increased total points by 1.7 on average (0.38 SD). However, when individuals had to defend the weak evidence position, being assigned to using ChatGPT had no positive e ect in performance.

Individuals using ChatGPT score on average 0.605 points more than control individuals (not signiﬁcant), and this is not a ected by whether they debated against a student in treatment (also using ChatGPT) or control group.

The probability of winning a debate is 10.5 percentage points higher (not signiﬁcant) when treatment individuals debate with a control group person. When treatment individuals compete against a treated student, their probability of winning is the same as that of control individuals (interaction e ect=-0.125).
Given my sample size of 142 students, I estimate the minimum detectable e ect size in 0.4SD at 90% conﬁdence levels.
ambiguity_or_caveats: The nature of the debate might be altered by the debating partners' performance, which could be a ected by the latter's treatment status. Even though my main speciﬁcations control for rival's treatment status, I also analyze the interaction between own treatment status and rival's treatment status to see whether the e ect of treatment is di erent when competing against another treated student during a debate. This is particularly relevant for winning the debate (if both treated students perform better, probability of winning might not rise for either of them).
This result has implications for the ongoing debate as regards to the potential risks of inadequate or misleading information that have been raised in the past relation to LLMs.

Given that previous research has pointed to problems of limited reliability of LLMs and invented references ( Bommasani et al. , 2021; Weidinger et al. , 2021), being able to discern good from bad information seems important to extract the beneﬁts of ChatGPT. One way of reading these results is that ChatGPT does provide relevant evidence, but extracting it e ectively from the AI crucially depends on one's ability. Having more information but little capacity to discriminate, might even lead to reduce productivity.
These ﬁndings highlight that a good analysis of the impact of GenAI on inequality will need to look at how GenAI interacts with speciﬁc tasks and users.

01a_quotes.notes: This is a preregistered RCT (AEA RCT Registry ID: AEARCTR-0011113) conducted at ESADE Business School and CUNEF University in March-April 2023. The study examines heterogeneous effects of ChatGPT access on debating performance across 142 undergraduate students, with particular focus on ability-based differences. The main finding contradicts the "productivity compression hypothesis" from prior GenAI literature: high-ability students benefit significantly more from ChatGPT than low-ability students in this task requiring higher-order skills and unpredictable interactions. The paper includes detailed mechanism analysis examining performance across five rubric dimensions and self-reported survey measures.

01_extraction.title: When GenAI increases inequality: evidence from a university debating competition
01_extraction.study_design_description: A randomized controlled trial (RCT) involving 142 undergraduate students at ESADE Business School and CUNEF University across three debating days in March-April 2023. Randomization was conducted at the individual level on each debating day, assigning participants to treatment and control groups, debating partners, and debating positions. After the first round (baseline), treatment students received a 20-minute ChatGPT training and were allowed to use ChatGPT throughout the remaining three to four rounds of debates, while control students could only use conventional internet resources. Each debate was audio recorded and evaluated by three independent expert judges using a standardized rubric.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Three to four rounds of short one-on-one debates on public policy topics over a three-hour session. Each debate consisted of 3 minutes for an opening statement and 2 minutes for refutation and conclusion per debater, with 15 minutes of preparation time per round. Students had approximately two hours of total debating time including preparation and actual debates.
01_extraction.population_description: 142 undergraduate university students from ESADE Business School and CUNEF University. Most participants were enrolled in law, business, or economics degrees, drawn primarily from two Debating courses at ESADE and an Economic Policy class at CUNEF. The first session (38 students) and second session (50 students) were conducted in English at ESADE on March 20 and 24, 2023; the third session (58 students) was conducted in Spanish on April 19, 2023. At the time of intervention, ChatGPT 3.5 had been available for approximately four months, and less than half of participating students reported prior ChatGPT use.

arms.description: After the first round, treatment students received a 20-minute intense training on ChatGPT by a ChatGPT heavy user and were allowed to use ChatGPT as support throughout the remaining three to four rounds of debates. Treatment and control students remained separated in two rooms during preparation. AI tools were explicitly prohibited for control students, and treatment students were explicitly told not to disclose ChatGPT use.
Control students received an extra 20-minute break instead of ChatGPT training and could only use conventional resources on the internet. AI tools were explicitly prohibited for control students.

outcomes.outcome_description: Individual debating points computed as the average score given by three independent judges to each participant for each round of debates. Judges awarded up to 50 points per debater following a rubric with five indicators: (1) clarity and validity of the defended position; (2) credibility of the evidence used; (3) formal quality and rhetoric; (4) ability to refute the rival's arguments; and (5) superiority of the arguments used. Each indicator received ten points.
Binary outcome indicating whether an individual won a debate. An individual is classified as winner if their average score (across three judges) in a debate was higher than that of their rival.
Rubric dimension measuring clarity and validity of the defended position, one of five standard categories evaluated by judges.
Rubric dimension measuring credibility of the evidence used, one of five standard categories evaluated by judges.
Rubric dimension measuring formal quality and rhetoric, one of five standard categories evaluated by judges.
Rubric dimension measuring ability to refute the rival's arguments, one of five standard categories evaluated by judges.
Rubric dimension measuring superiority of the arguments used, one of five standard categories evaluated by judges.
Self-reported perception of time needed to prepare the debates, measured via survey.
Self-reported perception of the usefulness of the tools used for the debate, measured via survey.

I also find a positive effect of the treatment on students' perceptions of the usefulness of the tools used for the debate: among those not on a scholarship, treatment individuals value the usefulness of materials 36 points higher than students having only internet access (or 1.07 standard deviations), and the effect was similar in size among those on a scholarship (albeit not significant).

01_extraction.notes: This is a preregistered RCT (AEA RCT Registry ID: AEARCTR-0011113). The study examines heterogeneous effects of ChatGPT access on debating performance across 142 undergraduate students (71 per arm), with particular focus on ability-based differences. The main finding contradicts the "productivity compression hypothesis" from prior GenAI literature: high-ability students benefit significantly more from ChatGPT than low-ability students in this task requiring higher-order skills and unpredictable interactions. The paper includes detailed mechanism analysis examining performance across five rubric dimensions and self-reported survey measures. The minimum detectable effect size at 90% confidence levels was estimated at 0.4 SD given the sample size of 142 students. Most reported effects are described as "not significant" or "insignificant" at conventional levels, but specific p-values are not provided in the quotes for most comparisons. The heterogeneous effects by ability level and scholarship status are described as "significant" or "significantly larger" in the quotes.

02_method.rationale: This is a randomized controlled trial conducted in a controlled academic setting with structured debate rounds, audio recording, and standardized evaluation rubrics. While randomization occurred ('Randomization was conducted at the individual level'), the highly controlled nature of the debate task with expert judges and standardized protocols makes this a lab task rather than a field experiment in a real work setting.

per_outcome.operationalization: Average score (0-50) from three independent judges using a rubric with five indicators: clarity, evidence credibility, rhetoric, refutation ability, and argument superiority
Binary indicator of whether participant's average judge score exceeded their rival's average score in a debate round
Judge-rated score (0-10) on clarity and validity of the defended position, one dimension of the debate rubric
Judge-rated score (0-10) on credibility of evidence used, one dimension of the debate rubric
Judge-rated score (0-10) on formal quality and rhetoric, one dimension of the debate rubric
Judge-rated score (0-10) on ability to refute rival's arguments, one dimension of the debate rubric
Judge-rated score (0-10) on superiority of arguments used, one dimension of the debate rubric
Self-reported perception of whether preparation time was sufficient, measured via post-experiment survey
Self-reported perception of tool usefulness for debate preparation, measured via post-experiment survey

04_onet.rationale: The participants are undergraduate students engaged in structured policy debates requiring them to develop original arguments, construct opening statements, and formulate refutations within time constraints. The task description emphasizes 'short one-on-one debates on public policy topics' with preparation and performance components that fundamentally require creative thinking, argumentation, and persuasive communication. This maps most directly to 'Thinking Creatively' as the core cognitive work activity being measured, rather than a specific occupation like lawyers or policy analysts, since the students are performing a bounded academic exercise focused on creative argumentation rather than the full scope of professional work.

alternates.onet_label: Communicating with Supervisors, Peers, or Subordinates

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI arm (ChatGPT treatment) vs control. Using the overall comparison (first row) as the primary effect.
llm_extracted.notes: The paper reports a standardized effect of 0.15 standard deviations for the overall treatment effect. The effect is described as not statistically significant (p-value not exactly reported, only stated as 'not statistically different from zero'). Multiple subgroup analyses are reported showing heterogeneous effects (e.g., 0.8 SD for high-ability students, 0.47 SD for top 50% baseline, 0.38 SD for strong evidence position), but the primary overall effect is 0.15 SD.
source_quotes: Treatment debaters are 9.2% more likely to win than control debaters and score on average 2.2% (equivalent to an increase by 0.15 standard deviations) higher than control individuals, but the differences are not statistically different from zero.

computed.notes: The paper reports a standardized effect of 0.15 standard deviations for the overall treatment effect. The effect is described as not statistically significant (p-value not exactly reported, only stated as 'not statistically different from zero'). Multiple subgroup analyses are reported showing heterogeneous effects (e.g., 0.8 SD for high-ability students, 0.47 SD for top 50% baseline, 0.38 SD for strong evidence position), but the primary overall effect is 0.15 SD.
